topic: Transitioning from individual variables to object literals and functional abstraction for managing multiple entities.

concepts.name: object literals
concepts.explanation: Grouping related properties (like x and y coordinates) into a single entity using curly braces `{}`. The instructor describes them as having 'key-value pairs' where the key is the name and the value is assigned using a colon.
concepts.why_it_matters: It prevents code bloat and makes it easier to manage many similar items without creating dozens of separate variables.

concepts.name: dot notation
concepts.explanation: Using a period to access the internal properties of an object, like `ufo1.x` or `object.s`.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows you to reach inside an object to read or change its specific data.

concepts.name: functional abstraction (passing objects as parameters)
concepts.explanation: Instead of passing individual numbers like `ufo(x, y)`, you pass the entire object as a single argument: `moveUfo(ufo)`. The function then uses the object's internal properties to do its work.
concepts.why_it_matters: It makes functions much more scalable and reusable. You can write one function that controls 100 different objects without changing the function code.

concepts.name: encapsulation of properties
concepts.explanation: Adding custom properties like `s` (speed) directly into the object literal so each entity can have its own unique behavior.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows every object to move at a different speed while still using the same logic.

code_examples.demonstrates: Creating an object literal with two properties (x and y) using key-value pairs.
code_examples.common_issue: Using an equal sign instead of a colon inside the braces.

code_examples.demonstrates: Using an object property to implement screen wrap-around logic.
A reusable function that takes an object as a parameter and updates its position based on its internal speed property.
code_examples.common_issue: Trying to pass individual values (x, y) instead of the whole object.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The code is getting longer and more 'messy' as you add more objects.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Using separate variables (x1, y1, x2, y2...) for every single property of every object.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Refactor the individual variables into object literals.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Objects fly off the screen and never come back.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Missing logic to reset the object's position when it exceeds the canvas width.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Add an `if` statement to check if `obj.x > width` and reset it.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The function doesn't know which object to move.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Passing specific values (x, y) instead of the whole object to a function designed for objects.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Pass the entire object as the parameter: `moveUfo(ufo1)`.

key_moments.description: The instructor notes that the current approach is 'getting more code rather than less,' creating a motivation to learn objects.
Tidy code is good code; organizing data into objects makes the script easier to read and maintain.
The pivotal shift from passing coordinates to passing the whole object, demonstrating true scalability.

student_questions: Why is my code getting so long and messy?
How do I group x and y together so they are easier to manage?
How can I make 100 objects move at different speeds without writing 100 functions?
What is the difference between a variable and an object property?
How do I use one function to move many different things?
Why am I getting errors when I try to pass my object into a function?
How do I make an object wrap around the screen?
How do I access a variable that is inside an object?
